summary: Joel Spolsky announces that Trello has been spun out of Fog Creek Software into its own independent company, Trello Inc., with outside investment from Index Ventures and Spark Capital. He recounts Trello's origin as one of four internal prototype projects in 2011, growing from a two-person team to 4.6 million users over three years. While Fog Creek didn't need outside money, the investment accelerates growth plans for features, sales, and marketing. Spolsky emphasizes that all three companies he's involved with—Fog Creek, Trello, and Stack Exchange—share the same 'operating system' of treating developers well and putting people first.
key_points: Trello grew from a two-person prototype team to 4.6 million users and its own company in three years, validating Fog Creek's internal incubation approach of running multiple small teams on product ideas

Trello succeeded as a non-developer product because its visual board/card metaphor made it instantly understandable to civilians, and it spreads virally as people adopt it from one context (reading group) into another (workplace)
Spolsky chose investors who already had stakes in Stack Exchange to avoid conflicts of interest, and only took outside money after achieving traction and revenue to maintain founder control

All three companies (Fog Creek, Trello, Stack Exchange) run on the same 'operating system' centered on private offices, developer autonomy, and treating people humanely—principles Spolsky spent a decade advocating on his blog
money_quotes: We peeled off eight developers. The idea was that they would break into four teams. Two people each. Each team would work for a few months building a prototype or MVP for some product idea. Hopefully, at least one of those ideas would turn into something people wanted.
It's a gas that expands to fill all available space. Somebody finds out about Trello from their reading group and adopts it at work; pretty soon their whole company has hundreds of Trello boards.
We didn't really need the money; Fog Creek is profitable and could afford to fund Trello development to profitability.

We didn't take any outside investment until we already had traction and revenue, so we could choose the investors that we thought were the most entrepreneur-friendly, and we kept control of the company.

They are all running basically the same operating system, based on the original microprocessor architecture known as 'making a company where the best developers want to work,' or, in simpler terms, treating people well.
Y'all don't listen, so I'm just going to have to build company after company that runs my own wacky operating system, and eventually you'll catch on.
It's OK to put people first. You don't have to be a psychopath or work people to death or create heaps of messy code or work in noisy open offices.
key_insight: The best way to grow successful products is to build companies that treat people well, take outside money only from a position of strength, and let great teams organically discover what users want.
